message.content:
Test file: src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/game-of-life.ts
Passing tests: 7 (all tests, none todo)
Recent changes: No implementation change for test 7 — the general survival+reproduction implementation already handled the blinker and negative coordinates.

This is the FINAL test. Do a thorough final-pass review of the whole implementation.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously. Apply Simple Design Rules / APP; attempt a refactoring if it genuinely improves the code, or document why none is warranted; keep all 7 tests green.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 7 tests green. Now the refactor phase analysis.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Going through every named element:

- `Cell` — clearly a coordinate pair. ✅
- `isNeighbor(a, b)` — returns whether two cells are adjacent (excluding identity). Name reveals intent. ✅
- `countLiveNeighbors(cell, cells)` — counts live cells adjacent to `cell`. ✅
- `survives(liveNeighbors)` — survival rule predicate (2 or 3). ✅
- `key(cell)` — produces a string identity key. ✅
- `neighborOffsets` — the 8 surrounding deltas. ✅
- `deadNeighborCandidates(cells)` — returns unique dead cells adjacent to live cells (candidates for birth). Accurate. ✅
- `nextGeneration(cells)` — the public entry. ✅

**Decision:** All names accurately reveal intent at the current full scope of behavior. No rename warranted.

## Step 2: Initial APP Mass

Whole-file breakdown:

- `isNeighbor`: bindings a,b,dx,dy + return expr. Math.abs×2 (4), abs args/subtractions, comparisons, &&/||. Roughly: bindings 4, invocations (abs×2=4, arithmetic/compares ~6 =12), constants ~4. ≈ 20
- `countLiveNeighbors`: bindings 2, filter+isNeighbor+length invocations (≈6), constant 0. ≈ 9
- `survives`: binding 1, compares 2 invocations (4), constants 2, || (2). ≈ 9
- `key`: binding 1, template invocation (2). ≈ 3
- `neighborOffsets`: 8 cells × 2 constants = 16 constants. ≈ 16
- `deadNeighborCandidates`: bindings (cells, live, candidates, deadByKey, x,y,dx,dy,cell) ~9, invocations (Set, map, flatMap, map, Map, filter, has, key×3, map, spread/values) heavy ~24, constant 1. ≈ 35
- `nextGeneration`: bindings 3, invocations (filter, survives, countLiveNeighbors, deadNeighborCandidates, filter, countLiveNeighbors, spread×2) ~16, conditional via === in filter, constant 3. ≈ 22

**Total Mass ≈ 114** (the exact figure matters less than relative comparison for candidate refactorings).

## Step 3: Simple Design Rules evaluation

**Rule 1 (Tests pass):** ✅ All 7 green.

**Rule 2 (Reveals intent):** The code reads top-down from primitives to the public function. One observation: `nextGeneration` calls `countLiveNeighbors(cell, cells)` in two places — once for survivors, once for born. This is a genuine conceptual duplication (the "count neighbors among the live set" idea), and it relates to Rule 3.

**Rule 3 (No duplication):**
- The literal `=== 3` birth check and the `survives` predicate both encode the neighbor-count rules. They're intentionally different rules (birth vs. survival), so not true duplication.
- `countLiveNeighbors(cell, cells)` is invoked from two call sites. The shared concept is "for each candidate cell, count its live neighbors and apply a rule." Both `survivors` and `born` follow the identical shape: `candidates.filter(cell => rule(countLiveNeighbors(cell, cells)))`. This is a real structural duplication worth examining.

**Rule 4 (Fewest elements):** No dead code or unnecessary abstractions. Each helper earns its place.

## Step 4: Candidate refactoring

The structural duplication in `nextGeneration` could be unified:

```ts
const liveNext = (candidates, rule) =>
  candidates.filter(cell => rule(countLiveNeighbors(cell, cells)));
```

Then `survivors = liveNext(cells, survives)` and `born = liveNext(deadCandidates, n => n === 3)`.

**Evaluating this against the rules:** This would remove the two-call-site structural similarity, but it introduces an inline `n => n === 3` predicate (less intent-revealing than the named `survives`) and a higher-order helper that adds an element (Rule 4) and indirection. The current two lines are each independently readable and state plainly what survivors and births are. Collapsing them trades clarity for marginal DRY. **Rule 2 trumps Rule 3 and APP here** — the abstraction would obscure intent more than the duplication harms it. I'll reject this one.

A second candidate: introduce a named `isBornCount`/`reproduces` predicate to mirror `survives`, replacing the magic `=== 3`:

```ts
const reproduces = (liveNeighbors: number): boolean => liveNeighbors === 3;
```

**Evaluating this:** This genuinely improves Rule 2 (reveals intent). Right now the survival rule is a named predicate (`survives`) but the birth rule is an inline magic literal (`=== 3`). That's an asymmetry: two parallel game rules expressed at different levels of abstraction. Naming the birth rule makes both Conway rules explicit and symmetric, and ties the literal `3` to its meaning. APP cost is tiny (one small binding + a constant, ≈3 mass) and the clarity gain is real. This is the strongest candidate — it improves the highest-priority applicable rule (Rule 2) without hurting any other.

Let me apply it.

input.old_string:
const survives = (liveNeighbors: number): boolean =>
  liveNeighbors === 2 || liveNeighbors === 3;

content.text:
## Step 5: New APP Mass

The change replaces an inline `=== 3` comparison (2 invocations + 1 constant ≈ 3 in `nextGeneration`) with an invocation of `reproduces` (2), and adds the `reproduces` definition (binding 1, compare-invocation 2, constant 1 ≈ 4). Net mass change ≈ **+3** (114 → ~117). This is the deliberate, acceptable Rule-2-over-APP trade: a tiny mass increase buys symmetric, named expression of both Conway rules.

## Step 6: Verify tests stay green

content.text:
🔄 Refactor Phase Complete (final pass):

**Naming Evaluation (first priority):** All existing names (`Cell`, `isNeighbor`, `countLiveNeighbors`, `survives`, `key`, `neighborOffsets`, `deadNeighborCandidates`, `nextGeneration`) accurately reveal intent at the current full scope. No renames warranted.

**Refactoring applied:** Extracted a named `reproduces(liveNeighbors)` predicate to replace the inline magic `=== 3` birth check in `nextGeneration`.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): The two Conway rules are now expressed symmetrically as named predicates — `survives` (2 or 3) and `reproduces` (exactly 3) — instead of one named rule plus one magic literal. The bare `3` is now tied to its meaning.

**Candidates considered and rejected (Rule 2 trumps Rule 3 / APP):**
- Collapsing the two `candidates.filter(cell => rule(countLiveNeighbors(cell, cells)))` call sites into a higher-order helper. Rejected: it would force an inline `n => n === 3` predicate and add an indirection element (Rule 4), obscuring intent more than the structural similarity costs.

**Mass change:** ≈114 → ≈117 (Δ +3). A deliberate, minor APP increase accepted in exchange for clearer, symmetric intent — consistent with "Rule 2 trumps APP."

**Tests:** All 7 passing ✅. `tsc --noEmit` clean ✅.

This was the final test. All tests are implemented and green; `experiment-done.txt` written with `DONE`.
